md: A **triangle** is a three-sided polygon — three sides, three angles. Triangles are the simplest polygon, and they're rigid: once you fix the lengths of the three sides, the shape is completely determined. That makes them the building block for nearly all geometry.

blocks.title: The angle-sum theorem
blocks.body: **The three angles of any triangle sum to exactly 180°.** No exceptions, regardless of size or shape.

md: Drag the vertices below and watch the three angles. The values change, but their sum stays at 180° — exactly.
(There can be only one right or obtuse angle — if there were two, their sum would already be ≥ 180°, leaving nothing for the third.)
**Equilateral**: all three sides equal length. (Then all three angles equal 60°.)
**Isosceles**: at least two sides equal. (The angles opposite the equal sides are equal too.)
**Scalene**: all three sides different lengths. (All angles different too.)

blocks.title: The triangle inequality
blocks.body: For any triangle with sides $a$, $b$, $c$: **the sum of any two sides must be greater than the third**. $a + b > c$, $b + c > a$, $a + c > b$. If those don't all hold, you can't form a triangle from those side lengths.

md: Two angles of a triangle measure 35° and 65°. What's the third angle?
Angle sum: $35 + 65 + x = 180 \Rightarrow x = 80°$. Since none of the three angles exceeds 90°, this is an **acute** triangle.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Three sides, three angles, **angles sum to 180°**. Classify by angles (acute/right/obtuse) and by sides (equilateral/isosceles/scalene). Triangle inequality: each side is less than the sum of the other two. These rules constrain everything else in geometry.
